Section heading: Our use of cookies

**Session 2022-23**

Section heading: Our use of cookies

**Session 2022-23**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by Peter Wright, Editor Emeritus, DMG Media (OSB47)**

**Online Safety Bill: the need for stronger protection for legitimate news content**

1.This submission is made on behalf of DMG Media, publisher of the Daily Mail, Mail on Sunday, Metro and i newspapers; MailOnline, metro.co.uk and inews.co.uk websites; and New Scientist magazine.

2.DMG Media has had serious concerns about the risk online safety legislation poses to freedom of expression in the UK ever since publication of the Online Harms white paper in 2019.

3.It has always seemed to us problematic that legislation which is intended to improve the publics’ experience of the internet potentially places the very US-owned monopolies that have created the problems it seeks to address in a position where they may believe they are obliged to censor what news content the British public can read.

4.One might have thought the legislation should be seeking to promote news content produced by responsible publishers which employ trained journalists, recognise their legal responsibilities, and submit to independent self-regulation.

5.In our view it is inevitable that online platforms, faced with draconian fines and personal criminal liability, and obliged under the duty of care to perform an impossible task in judging what is legal harmful, will try to protect themselves by setting their algorithms to block or restrict access to any content which appears to pose any risk.
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by Peter Wright, Editor Emeritus, DMG Media (OSB47)**

**Online Safety Bill: the need for stronger protection for legitimate news content**

1.This submission is made on behalf of DMG Media, publisher of the Daily Mail, Mail on Sunday, Metro and i newspapers; MailOnline, metro.co.uk and inews.co.uk websites; and New Scientist magazine.

2.DMG Media has had serious concerns about the risk online safety legislation poses to freedom of expression in the UK ever since publication of the Online Harms white paper in 2019.

3.It has always seemed to us problematic that legislation which is intended to improve the publics’ experience of the internet potentially places the very US-owned monopolies that have created the problems it seeks to address in a position where they may believe they are obliged to censor what news content the British public can read.

4.One might have thought the legislation should be seeking to promote news content produced by responsible publishers which employ trained journalists, recognise their legal responsibilities, and submit to independent self-regulation.

5.In our view it is inevitable that online platforms, faced with draconian fines and personal criminal liability, and obliged under the duty of care to perform an impossible task in judging what is legal harmful, will try to protect themselves by setting their algorithms to block or restrict access to any content which appears to pose any risk.

chunks.text: platforms, faced with draconian fines and personal criminal liability, and obliged under the duty of care to perform an impossible task in judging what is legal harmful, will try to protect themselves by setting their algorithms to block or restrict access to any content which appears to pose any risk. 6.It is in the nature of news that it covers controversial subjects – terrorism, suicide, self-harm and child sexual exploitation, to name but a few. As news publishers know from their own experience with the platforms, algorithms are blunt instruments at best, and have particular difficulty understanding context. It is highly unlikely that an algorithm could reliably distinguish between a news report containing video of a terror attack and a post on a terrorist website glorying in the same incident. 7.We are also concerned that the vague definition of ‘legal but harmful’ will provide an opportunity for lobby groups to attempt to censor any point of view with which they disagree. Yet surely the whole point of media plurality is to ensure contrarian views are heard. 8.For all these reasons we have argued from the beginning for a complete exemption from the Online Safety Bill for news publishers. When the draft Bill was published we were pleased to see that our own websites were out scope, and the duty of care did not apply to content from recognised news publishers.
chunks.display_text: 6.It is in the nature of news that it covers controversial subjects – terrorism, suicide, self-harm and child sexual exploitation, to name but a few. As news publishers know from their own experience with the platforms, algorithms are blunt instruments at best, and have particular difficulty understanding context. It is highly unlikely that an algorithm could reliably distinguish between a news report containing video of a terror attack and a post on a terrorist website glorying in the same incident. 7.We are also concerned that the vague definition of ‘legal but harmful’ will provide an opportunity for lobby groups to attempt to censor any point of view with which they disagree. Yet surely the whole point of media plurality is to ensure contrarian views are heard. 8.For all these reasons we have argued from the beginning for a complete exemption from the Online Safety Bill for news publishers. When the draft Bill was published we were pleased to see that our own websites were out scope, and the duty of care did not apply to content from recognised news publishers.

chunks.text: we have argued from the beginning for a complete exemption from the Online Safety Bill for news publishers. When the draft Bill was published we were pleased to see that our own websites were out scope, and the duty of care did not apply to content from recognised news publishers. 9.However, there was still a problem, in that the Bill creates very powerful incentives for platforms to set their algorithms to block or restrict content which addresses certain subject matters, as yet not clearly defined. Without a prohibition on blocking news content, it is highly likely platforms will take the easiest path, which would be to block all content on such subjects, whoever has produced it. The authors of the Bill did recognise that this was a problem, but their remedy – a right of appeal to the platform – is very weak. 10.The shelf-life of an online news story is extremely short – a matter of hours at most. There would be no point in an appeals process which might take weeks. In the unlikely event of a platform deciding it had made a mistake, there would be no value in reinstating a news story which had ceased to be topical some weeks earlier. 11.We made our case to the Joint Committee of both Houses which scrutinised the Bill, and the Committee recommended an exemption along the lines we had previously suggested: *We recommend that the news publisher content exemption is strengthened to include a requirement that news publisher content should not be moderated, restricted or removed unless it is content the publication of which clearly constitutes a criminal offence, or which has been found to be unlawful by order of a court within the appropriate jurisdiction.\[1\]*
chunks.display_text: 9.However, there was still a problem, in that the Bill creates very powerful incentives for platforms to set their algorithms to block or restrict content which addresses certain subject matters, as yet not clearly defined. Without a prohibition on blocking news content, it is highly likely platforms will take the easiest path, which would be to block all content on such subjects, whoever has produced it. The authors of the Bill did recognise that this was a problem, but their remedy – a right of appeal to the platform – is very weak. 10.The shelf-life of an online news story is extremely short – a matter of hours at most. There would be no point in an appeals process which might take weeks. In the unlikely event of a platform deciding it had made a mistake, there would be no value in reinstating a news story which had ceased to be topical some weeks earlier. 11.We made our case to the Joint Committee of both Houses which scrutinised the Bill, and the Committee recommended an exemption along the lines we had previously suggested: *We recommend that the news publisher content exemption is strengthened to include a requirement that news publisher content should not be moderated, restricted or removed unless it is content the publication of which clearly constitutes a criminal offence, or which has been found to be unlawful by order of a court within the appropriate jurisdiction.\[1\]*

chunks.text: publisher content exemption is strengthened to include a requirement that news publisher content should not be moderated, restricted or removed unless it is content the publication of which clearly constitutes a criminal offence, or which has been found to be unlawful by order of a court within the appropriate jurisdiction.\[1\]* 12.When the substantive Bill was published the government did not adopt this recommendation. However the Culture Secretary did promise in March that the Government would put forward an amendment to the Bill which would require platforms to notify publishers if they plan to block or restrict content from recognised ness publishers, and keep it online until any appeal has been heard. This how she described it in a television interview \[2\]: *If… platforms remove something which a journalist or a news outlet puts online, they have to notify that journalist that they're about to remove that content, they have to say why they're going to remove it, and they have to give the journalist the right to appeal, and the content remains online while that happens.* 13.This was reiterated by Under-Secretary of State Chris Philp during the Second Reading of the Bill \[3\]:
chunks.display_text: 12.When the substantive Bill was published the government did not adopt this recommendation. However the Culture Secretary did promise in March that the Government would put forward an amendment to the Bill which would require platforms to notify publishers if they plan to block or restrict content from recognised ness publishers, and keep it online until any appeal has been heard. This how she described it in a television interview \[2\]: *If… platforms remove something which a journalist or a news outlet puts online, they have to notify that journalist that they're about to remove that content, they have to say why they're going to remove it, and they have to give the journalist the right to appeal, and the content remains online while that happens.* 13.This was reiterated by Under-Secretary of State Chris Philp during the Second Reading of the Bill \[3\]:

chunks.text: to remove that content, they have to say why they're going to remove it, and they have to give the journalist the right to appeal, and the content remains online while that happens.* 13.This was reiterated by Under-Secretary of State Chris Philp during the Second Reading of the Bill \[3\]: *The Bill also enshrines, for the first time, free speech-something that we all feel very strongly about-but it goes beyond that. As well as enshrining free speech in clause 19, it gives special protection, in clauses 15 and 16, for content of journalistic and democratic importance. As my right hon. Friend the Secretary of State indicated in opening the debate, we intend to table a Government amendment-a point that my right hon. Friends the Members for Maldon and for Ashford (Damian Green) asked me to confirm-to make sure that journalistic content cannot be removed until a proper right of appeal has taken place. I am pleased to confirm that now.* 14.We have been assured this is still the Government’s intention, but as yet no draft of the amendment has been published. Unfortunately, as Owen Meredith, the CEO of our trade body, the News Media Association, made clear when he gave evidence to yesterday’s meeting of the Bill Committee, this means that as the Bill stands, it remains a serious threat to the freedom of the Press in the UK \[4\]. *Although the Government’s stated intention is to have recognised news media content outside the scope of the Bill, we don’t believe the drafting as was, and still is, actually achieves that.*
chunks.display_text: *The Bill also enshrines, for the first time, free speech-something that we all feel very strongly about-but it goes beyond that. As well as enshrining free speech in clause 19, it gives special protection, in clauses 15 and 16, for content of journalistic and democratic importance. As my right hon. Friend the Secretary of State indicated in opening the debate, we intend to table a Government amendment-a point that my right hon. Friends the Members for Maldon and for Ashford (Damian Green) asked me to confirm-to make sure that journalistic content cannot be removed until a proper right of appeal has taken place. I am pleased to confirm that now.* 14.We have been assured this is still the Government’s intention, but as yet no draft of the amendment has been published. Unfortunately, as Owen Meredith, the CEO of our trade body, the News Media Association, made clear when he gave evidence to yesterday’s meeting of the Bill Committee, this means that as the Bill stands, it remains a serious threat to the freedom of the Press in the UK \[4\]. *Although the Government’s stated intention is to have recognised news media content outside the scope of the Bill, we don’t believe the drafting as was, and still is, actually achieves that.*

chunks.text: as the Bill stands, it remains a serious threat to the freedom of the Press in the UK \[4\]. *Although the Government’s stated intention is to have recognised news media content outside the scope of the Bill, we don’t believe the drafting as was, and still is, actually achieves that.* *Ministers and the secretary of state have confirmed both in public appearances and the second reading of the Bill that they wish to table further amendments to achieve the aims the Government set out, which is to make sure content from recognised news publishers is fully out of scope of the Bill.’* 15.We do not doubt the Government’s intentions, but this is novel legislation, attempting for the first time to regulate the digital universe without undermining the right to free speech, and if the drafting is not right the risk of unintended consequences is high. 16.It is vitally important that news publishers, who cannot perform their role in society without genuine and unrestricted press freedom, have time to consider these amendments carefully before they are included in the Bill. We are aware that the Committee is due to complete its scrutiny by June 30. We hope it can use its good offices to persuade the DCMS to share its drafting well before then. **Peter Wright** **Editor Emeritus** **DMG Media** *May 2022* --- \[1\] https://publications.parliament.uk/pa/jt5802/jtselect/jtonlinesafety/129/12910.htm#\_idTextAnchor178 para 304 \[2\] https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-10621259/Nadine-Dorries-preserve-freedom-Press-online.html \[3\] https://hansard.parliament.uk/commons/2022-04-19/debates/F88B42D3-BFC4-4612-B166-8D2C15FA3E4E/OnlineSafetyBill#contribution-26BCF9F0-99DA-4358-B857-C562025B9490 \[4\] https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-10859231/Act-protect-freedom-Press-MPs-urged.html

We are aware that the Committee is due to complete its scrutiny by June 30. We hope it can use its good offices to persuade the DCMS to share its drafting well before then. **Peter Wright** **Editor Emeritus** **DMG Media** *May 2022* --- \[1\] https://publications.parliament.uk/pa/jt5802/jtselect/jtonlinesafety/129/12910.htm#\_idTextAnchor178 para 304 \[2\] https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-10621259/Nadine-Dorries-preserve-freedom-Press-online.html \[3\] https://hansard.parliament.uk/commons/2022-04-19/debates/F88B42D3-BFC4-4612-B166-8D2C15FA3E4E/OnlineSafetyBill#contribution-26BCF9F0-99DA-4358-B857-C562025B9490 \[4\] https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-10859231/Act-protect-freedom-Press-MPs-urged.html
